analysis:
- Strong patterns (Heuristics 1–5, 7–9): Pareto prefiltering + cheap rect-volume (single-point) surrogate → adaptive shortlist → stamped lazy-greedy heap that only recomputes true marginal HV for the heap top → HV caching → small bounded swap refinement. This structure minimizes expensive HV calls while keeping quality high. Using pygmo when present gives exact HV cheaply; Monte‑Carlo fallbacks trade accuracy for time.
- Effective engineering choices: adaptive shortlist sizes (scale with k), normalized surrogates for stable seeding, stamp-based heap entries to avoid global recomputation, and caching HVs keyed by set identities. Local single-swap hillclimb yields consistent incremental improvements with bounded cost.
- Common inefficiencies in weaker variants (10–12 and some lower-ranked versions): large Monte‑Carlo dominated matrices (M×N) or very large sample budgets blow memory/time; naïve per-iteration nondomination checks and repeated conversion/stacking of arrays (rebuilding arrays for every HV call) add overhead; recomputing HV(S) from scratch too often rather than using cached values increases cost; heavy pairwise distance computations for diversity can be expensive and often unnecessary; oversized shortlists and unbounded swap loops harm runtime without proportional HV gain.
- Micro‑optimizations observed: using tuples vs frozensets affects key construction cost; mapping candidate indices to a compact contiguous pool reduces array slicing overhead; precomputing per-point rects and reusing them avoids repeated prod operations; deterministic stamping and incremental hv bookkeeping prevent redundant exact computations.
- Tradeoffs: stochastic sampling (Heuristic 1) aids exploration and avoids surrogate bias but requires careful seeding and cache reuse to not explode HV evaluations. Diversity-aware scoring (Heuristics 5–6) gains spread but at cost of pairwise work; keep diversity cheap (approximate sampling or clustering) if used.
- Design consistency issues: inconsistent reference handling across codes (some clamp ref to max+eps, others scale by 1.1) can change candidate validity—standardize early. Also prefer compact keys (sorted tuple) and small integer-based pools to reduce hashing/iteration overhead.
experience: Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.
current_reflection: Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.
good_reflections: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates and remove points dominated by the reference. Use lazy greedy with a priority queue storing marginal HV contributions and only recompute when a candidate reaches the top. Cache incremental HV or use per‑point contribution routines instead of full recompute. Combine a cheap surrogate (R2) to shortlist candidates, then refine top ones with exact HV/local swaps. Vectorize mask math and minimize Python loops.

Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.

Always Pareto‑filter first, then shortlist by single‑point rect volumes plus a lightweight diversity pick. Use a stamped lazy greedy heap with exact HV from a fast C library; cache marginal gains keyed by compact bitmasks. Limit Monte‑Carlo to large‑D fallback with small M. Cap swap neighborhood and parallelize exact evaluations. Vectorize dominance checks and avoid hot Python loops or frequent frozenset creation.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto prefilter, stamped lazy‑greedy, rect surrogate shortlist (c·k), HV cache, pygmo or shared MC fallback, compact index pool, bounded local swaps.
- Advice: Map indices to a compact pool, shortlist by single‑point rect volumes (adaptive c·k), run stamped lazy heap recomputing exact marginal HV only at heap top, cache HVs (bitmask/tuple keys), use pygmo if present else small shared Monte‑Carlo, apply tiny adaptive swap budget.
- Avoid: Frozenset-heavy keys, full HV recompute for every candidate, unbounded MC/diversity trials, Python loops in dominance/HV hot paths.
- Explanation: This maximizes hypervolume per compute by cheap filtering, selective exact evals, vectorized dominance math, and bounded local search.
